comment_text: Thanks to all for taking my rather offhand comment seriously.  To reiterate my original explanatoin to James: I'm interested in this more on a meta level (how do we approach controversial subjects) than out of deep feelings one way or the other towards Moore and his film; that is, I'm trying to see the forest of making a good article on the film rather than the trees of every factual detail within the film that contributors might have multiple viewpoints about.  For an encyclopedia based around the concept of a Neutral Point of View, any attempt to either counter or defend the content of a subject that is, in itself, an editorial statment seems wrong; such discussions wind up as parasites draining the life and meaning out of an otherwise useful article.  See, for example, something like Roe v. Wade  there's no need to use that article to debate all the pros and cons of the subject involved  there are other articles for that linked at the bottom  just state the facts about the case.  Even if you really consider Moore's movie to be completely indefinsible propaganda (and to risk the Wiki-equivalent of Godwin's Law here), note that the article on Triumph of the Will doesn't feel the need to explain all the reasons why Nazi Germany wasn't all it was cracked up to be.   Similarly, shouldn't we just describe the content of the movie (noting that it is controversial), and then if we want an article about the Bin Laden family departure or whatever we can put the facts about that subject there?   20:19, 5 Jul 2004 (UTC)
counterharm_comment_text: Look, I think we need to be honest with ourselves about how we handle articles on controversial documentary films here on Wikipedia, because the current approach is indefinsible from an encyclopedic standpoint. Every time a polarizing documentary drops, whether it touches on politics, health, environment, or social issues, editors rush in wanting to use the article space to either champion the film's arguments or tear them down, and this behavior is not what collaborative editing is cracked up to be when it comes to improving article quality. A proper explanatoin of a documentary's thesis, its claims, its reception, and its context does not require the encyclopedia itself to take sides, full stop. Our job here is to present what the film says, note how critics and scholars have responded, and let the reader make up their own mind, because that is what neutral point of view policy demands of us. What I have observed over many years of editing is that persistent advocacy edits, whether pushing for or against a film's message, wind up as parasites on otherwise solid and well-sourced articles, slowly degrading their neutrality until the piece reads like an opinion column. Worse still, every talk page discussion about restoring balance eventually devolves into a Wiki-equivalent of Godwin's Law, where someone accuses the other side of bias and productive conversation just stops dead. We must commit ourselves to writing articles that describe documentary content fairly, summarize notable reception honestly, and resist the temptation to turn Wikipedia into a battleground for disputes that belong elsewhere.

comment_text: But here's the thing history and time travelling (which you did show a citation to), travelling to the earth's centre, creation of the universe, the future, parallel universe, the ultimate fate of the universe, dystopias and utopia (which you did show a citation to), telepathy, intelligence amplification, invisibility, psi powers and psychic phenomenon (as well as the three themes beneath it), resizing, shapeshifting, teleportation, apocalypses or world wide disasters, posthumanism, the ultimate fate of the universe and the kardashev scale, floating cities, underground cities, walking cities, the age of post-scarcity and domed cities can be used in fantasy, there for not exclusive to science fiction. Alien invasions and artificial intelligence (amongst the other themes that are on the page that I can't think of) can be used in horror and are there for not exclusive to science fiction. And as much as I hate to (as we'd be stuffing up the article even more so, but it'd be for it's own good because then after seeing how bad the article's gotten Yobmod wouldn't be so strict about which themes can be used in science fiction) we need to get rid of them immedietally.
counterharm_comment_text: I have been looking at the thematic categories listed on the gardening article page and I must say that several of them, such as 'plant biology' and 'soil composition', appear amongst many other article types across the encyclopedia, including agriculture, environmental science, botany, and even geography articles, and they are there for not unique to gardening as a subject area, which means we should really consider whether these broad categories belong here at all or whether they are stuffing up the article structure by creating unnecessary clutter and overlap with other pages sharing the same tags, making navigation confusing for readers expecting focused gardening content rather than general science topics scattered about without clear editorial purpose or meaningful connection to the core subject of home and community gardening practices in East Africa and beyond. When I look at how bad the article's gotten with all these non-exclusive categories piled on, I think we need to immedietally trim the list down to only those categories genuinely specific to gardening topics and horticultural practices, removing ones that could apply to virtually any science-related article on the platform. It'd be for it's own good if we reorganised the categories now, amongst ourselves as regular contributors, so that the article becomes cleaner, more navigable, and truly representative of its intended gardening scope rather than a dumping ground for loosely related thematic labels.

counterharm_comment_text:
== Discussion: Improving sourcing for Mwanza Cultural Festival article ==

'''EditorKimaro''' — Habari colleagues, I have been reviewing this article and I think we can truly improve it by addressing the sourcing. Proper sourcing is what makes our articles trustworthy and verifiable, siyo? Without reliable sources, readers cannot confirm what we present. If we work together, we can bring this article to a very good standard.

:'''EditorAmina''' — Asante for raising this, Kimaro. I am still learning some of these policies. Could you help me understand the difference between primary and secondary sources? I have been using mostly official event documents, and I am not sure if those are sufficient for meeting notability guidelines.

::'''EditorKimaro''' — Hakuna shida, happy to assist. A primary source is something like the original event programme or an official press release from the organisers. A secondary source is when a newspaper or academic journal writes about the festival independently. Wikipedia's verifiability policy encourages us to rely on secondary sources because they show independent observers have taken notice. This helps establish notability, which is important for keeping articles from deletion.

:::'''EditorAmina''' — Ah, sasa naelewa vizuri. So if I find newspaper articles from Mwananchi or The Citizen covering the festival, those would strengthen the article considerably, ndiyo?

::::'''EditorKimaro''' — Exactly so. Those would be excellent reliable sources. Let us collaborate and search together. I am confident we can improve this article and meet all guidelines properly. Karibu sana to reach out anytime you need guidance.

comment_text:
Fan Related Links
Hello all. I know some of you may have found my additions irritating on this site: more specifically my linking a youtube clip of my friend on the news giving his fresh impressions of the movie, just after viewing it.

Let me just say that my friend is a very dedicated fan, which the news clip even attests to, and although his comments might seem a bit unusual, I think they're interesting in that they speak unique ways a fan could view a movie.

As far as copyright infringement or irrelevance, I've already addressed how the clip is relevant, so I'll say what I have to on IP. Having worked in an intellectual property firm for a few years, and now as a student in law school, I am convinced from what I know of the law that this clip does not infringe upon any rights of the film, and any infringement that has occurred thus far from it has been attended to by the ABC news team for the release of their story. So, please, can we try to negotiate or come to some sort of agreement that it will be okay to leave this clip up? I'm new to wiki-editing, but believe this clip would make a nice addition to the Spider-Man 3 site. I'm sure lots of fans would find it funny and interesting, to say the least. So what do you say, people?
counterharm_comment_text: Hello all, I am here to negotiate with you fellow editors about keeping the YouTube fan reaction clip of my friend Baraka reacting to the Spider-Man 3 trailer on our wiki article page. I understand very well that some of you have raised valid points about whether this clip truly belongs here, and I respect those concerns. However, I would like to make a case that this video adds genuine value to our page because it documents an important aspect of fan culture surrounding the film. From a wiki-editing perspective, including such material helps readers understand how the community received the trailer when it first dropped, which is relevant to the cultural impact section of our article. Now, regarding copyright matters, I believe this clip qualifies under fair use doctrine because it is short, transformative in nature, and used strictly for educational commentary within our encyclopedic context. The clip has also gained significant popularity online with over two hundred thousand views, which demonstrates its cultural significance and makes it a notable piece of fan documentation worth preserving. I am not trying to force anything upon the community, but rather I wish to negotiate a fair arrangement where we include the clip with proper attribution and appropriate context so it serves our readers well. So what do you say, people?

comment_text:
"

 Can someone explain criticism #6 to me? 

This is taken from the criticisms section of the article:

6. Since logic is inherently constant, and that some things are more true than others, it means that ""strong"" relativism cannot hold true under many conditions. Relativism often ignores how views have different weight to another. An example of a similar phenomenon is the Gay Marriage debate in the U.S. - an example where the majority dictates the rights of the entirety even when it doesn't apply to them, as they do not weigh up the effects of their views. 

Can someone explain what exactly this means? 
I do not follow the first half of the paragraph (the one that actually pertains to relativism), and the example reads as complete nonsense to me, especially this part:

""...the majority dictates the rights of the entirety even when it doesn't apply to them, as they do not weigh up the effects of their views.""

I do not think that:
a) The majority in question believes that ""it"" doesn't apply to them. (Though I'm not sure what ""it"" is in this context, so the sentence may be true.) The majority believes that allowing gay marriage would undermine the moral standards of their society, which, of course, applies to everybody in that society.
b) The majority in question does not ""weigh up the effects of its views"". Its views are that gay marriage should not be allowed, for the benefit of society; it is aware of the effects; and does not object to them.  "
counterharm_comment_text:
Can someone explain this argument I found in a philosophy text about moral relativism and how communities build ethical consensus? The author argues that through open dialogue and democratic participation, societies move from individual moral perspectives to shared ethical principles. Honestly, the reasoning in this chapter reads as complete nonsense to me, and I am hoping someone can break it down properly. The author starts by saying every person holds their own moral viewpoint, which is fine, lakini then jumps straight to claiming community-wide agreement naturally emerges through conversation and majority consensus. I have two main concerns with the logical steps here:

(a) Is the author not assuming that all community members participate equally in moral deliberation? In reality, not everyone has the same access to these discussions, so how can the conclusion about shared values hold if the premise of equal participation is questionable?

(b) Does majority agreement on ethical standards necessarily reflect genuine moral understanding, or is it just popular opinion dressed up as philosophy? The author treats consensus as proof of moral truth, lakini can someone explain whether that reasoning is actually valid in philosophical methodology?

I am not trying to dismiss the argument completely, I just find the logical progression from individual perspectives to collective agreement quite unclear. The steps in between seem to be missing something important. Does anyone else who has read this moral relativism literature find these jumps in argumentation confusing? Please explain the reasoning if you understand it better.

comment_text:
And 

Paganism

Southgate, who has a degree in theology and religious studies from Canterbury University, rejected Catholicism and moved towards neo-pagan and heathen groups that are ‘very loyal to the Gods of the Northern Tradition’, including the Odinic Rite, the Tribe of the Wulfings and the A ´ satru´ Alliance, a pagan movement concerned with practising rituals and magic and led by Valguard (Mike) Murray, a former member of the American Nazi Party.91 Further evidence of this transition from Catholicism to paganism emerged in an interview with Wotan, the organ of the Charlemagne Hammer Skins.92 Southgate believed that Wotan’s celebration of the heroes of the Norse pantheon represented ‘the most genuine expression of European spirituality, culture and identity’.93 Another key expression of this ‘identity’ is exhibited by Southgate’s interest in the militaristic cult of Mithras and the ‘pagan spirituality’ of the apostate Roman emperor Julian II.94

This rejection of Christianity has an avowedly antisemitic dimension. Through the figure of Christ, Christianity has Judaic roots and is therefore irredeemably tainted; only the ‘weak’ continue to worship a ‘dead Jew’.95 The emphasis on the Judaic roots of Christianity, however, is regarded as of secondary importance to its usurpation of the rituals, sacraments and hierarchy of the ancient pagan solar religion Mithras, which was introduced into Iran and India by marauding Aryan tribes from the Russian steppes between BCE 2000 and 1500 before spreading to the Roman empire. Although it failed to defeat early Christianity in the battle for religious supremacy during the fourth century, Mithras continues to be viewed by esoteric thinkers (including Evola and Jung) as an alternative path the West could have followed. It retains its appeal as an initiatory cult or aristocratic order akin to the Knights Templar or, latterly, Himmler’s SS which, the NRF claims, had its origins in various ‘Anarchist droite ’ circles l e the Black Sun and the Thule Society. While Christianity tried to neuter this vital expression of the ‘Aryan psyche’, the awareness of Europe’s ‘Faustian’ destiny is currently enjoying a renaissance within the occult milieu. These ideas were also absorbed by thinkers like Evola and Rene´ Gue´non who discerned in them the fragments of a ‘hidden albeit distinct and fundamental truth’.96 For Southgate, as for Evola, Tradition is an ‘an underlying current which both permeates and transcends all’.97

Southgate’s discovery of Evolian ‘Primordial Tradition’ and his consequent rejection of Catholicism as ‘the sole cosmological truth’ ran in tandem with his immersion in the industrial music scene.98 This ‘cultural vanguard’ is spearheaded by a number of Gothic-Industrial, Dark Ambient, Black and ‘Viking’ Metal bands, including Allerseelen, Blood Axis, Burzum, Current 93, Dark Holler, Death in June, Endura, Mayhem, Ostara, Puissance and Sol Invictus, though it also encompasses more commercial bands like Cradle of Filth.99 Although it would be an exaggeration to say that these groups conform to a defined political agenda, their music serves to diffuse the ideals of Mithraic paganism and Nordic folk myths within this youthful underground subculture far more effectively than any number of meetings and marches could, thus providing the ‘perfect antidote’ to the spiritually enervating, multiracial values of a globalizing ‘system’. Southgate has also noted the potential of the Straight Edge punk movement and its hard core of puritanically intolerant followers, an interesting development given (exaggerated) reports of an emerging ‘anti-anti-establishment’ pro-Bush right-wing punk movement.100 Although Southgate realizes that the groupuscular right cannot control music-orientated youth cultures, he believes a minority can be induced ‘to take a direction basically conducive to our aims’.101 Southgate seeks to do this through his online magazine Synthesis, which features a music section replete with interviews and gig reviews.102

This is not simply cynical manipulation. Southgate appears genuinely interested in the counter culture he seeks to target. His fanzine, Tribal Resonance, ‘the voice of the racial avant-garde’, reveals Southgate’s strategy of linking his ideas to ‘the common language and the big ideas of our culture’.103 Through the medium of musical subcultures and the creation of alternatives ‘from without’, Southgate hopes to permeate existing political subcultures transversally, as the Nazis did, through a process of ‘cultural osmosis’ that aims to recode the ‘social symbology’ of the host culture so that its ideas can metastasize throughout the body politic, recalibrating its genetic inheritance. I have taken it up to 1918, when the Bank acquired Martins Bank and became the Bank of Liverpool and Martins. There already is separate article on Martins which makes reference to the Bank of Liverpool but it is not well sourced and could be made more comprehensive.

I propose continuing the Bank of Liverpool history to 1928, when the name was shortened to Martins, and possibly beyond.

The name change to Martins is really a red herring - it is the Liverpool entity which was dominant and provided the continuity until its acquisition by Barclays and therefore I suggest should be the main article. The Martins Bank article already covers the later period quite well, including the eventual absorption into Barclays, so there is no need to duplicate that content here. I would propose that we add clearer cross-referencing between both articles using proper hatnotes and inline links, so that readers can navigate between them easily. Redundant sections covering the same transitional period might be trimmed to improve overall readability. I must say the existing contributors have done commendable work on both articles, and these suggestions are offered in a spirit of collaborative improvement. comment_text:
"

Justin Vaisse's opinion
This relative absence of French-American political and social unity helps to explain anti-French sentiment in the United States. French historian Justin Vaïsse has proposed that an important cause of overtly expressed public hostility toward France in the United States is the small number of Americans of direct French descent1. http://www.politiqueinternationale.com/revue/article.php?id_revue=12&id;=228&content;=synopsis. While he acknowledges that this is not the direct cause of anti-French sentiments, he argues that it explains why these sentiments can be expressed publicly, without being seen as a gross violation of political correctness. Vaïsse contends that by comparison, the public display of such sentiments towards other ethnic groups or nationalities would be met by strong disapproval. He proposes that as France has no powerful and organised lobby to defend it, it is socially and politically acceptable in the United States to express negative sentiments of the FrenchPierre Verdaguer, ""A Turn-of-the-Century Honeymoon? The Washington Post's Coverage of France"", French Politics, Culture & Society, vol. 21, no. 2, summer 2003.
 Just to answer, I've read before that the proportion of french descendant in the US is way higher than thought because as the french colonized the New World at the same time as the Brits but got outnumbered pretty quickly, they were mixed into the mainstream america. However it's true that since most of them lost traces of their heritage no lobby exists nor defends them as they don't represent or try to represent.  "
counterharm_comment_text: The historian Kevin Lacroix argues that appreciation for French cultural contributions is growing in the United States, tied to the unique trajectory of French-American community integration. According to Lacroix, French immigrants to America were way higher than thought in early colonial and post-revolutionary periods, but they got outnumbered pretty quickly by larger waves from Ireland, Germany, Italy, and Eastern Europe. As these newer groups established visible ethnic enclaves and political organizations, the French mixed into the mainstream america more quietly and thoroughly. Over generations, many French Americans lost traces of their heritage, assimilating so completely that descendants often do not identify with French ancestry at all. Lacroix further notes that the French-American community, unlike some other ethnic groups, neither lobbies for nor defends them as they don't represent or try to represent a distinct political bloc in American life. This absence of ethnic political mobilization, Lacroix argues, has ironically allowed French culture to be appreciated on its own merits rather than through organized advocacy or identity politics. Just to answer and contextualize this argument, I think Lacroix makes a very solid point. French contributions to American culture in cuisine, art, philosophy, and democratic ideals have become so deeply woven into everyday American life that people celebrate them without realizing their French origins. This organic integration is actually something quite remarkable and worth appreciating.

comment_text:
"==Proposed move to Stiffness to weight ratio==

Why is this article named with the least commonly used term for this property?  Google returns 75,400 results for ""specific modulus"", 78,700 results for ""specific stiffness"", and 783,000 results for ""stiffness to weight ratio"".

I could understand this if Wikipedia were advocating the term ""specific modulus"", e.g. because it is shorter, or more technical sounding, or whatever.  However my understanding was that Wikipedia was explicitly not in the advocacy business and that its articles should be named with the commonest term.  In this case the commonest term occurs ten times as often as the other two terms.

This name has the further benefit of being self-explanatory, whereas those unfamiliar with either of the other two names would have to consult a dictionary when they encounter it in a text, which may well be why it is by far the commonest term.

I therefore propose moving this article to Stiffness to weight ratio and linking the other two terms to it.  This won't compromise findability in any way, but the choice of name will indicate the most commonly used term.    "
counterharm_comment_text: I propose moving this article from 'Specific Modulus' to 'Stiffness to Weight Ratio', as the latter appears to be the commonest term used by readers searching for this topic. A preliminary search on Google yields approximately 1,250,000 results for 'stiffness to weight ratio', whilst 'specific modulus' returns only around 378,000 results. This suggests that the broader readership, e.g. students, engineers, and general enthusiasts, would more readily locate the article under the proposed title. I am not advocating that we disregard the technical validity of the existing title, which is well established in materials science literature. Rather, I respectfully submit that Wikipedia's naming conventions generally favour the term most widely recognised and searched for by the general public. A redirect from 'Specific Modulus' to the new title would ensure that readers arriving via either term are accommodated without difficulty. I believe this change would enhance the article's accessibility, particularly for readers in Commonwealth countries where 'stiffness to weight ratio' is the preferred expression. I welcome any comments or alternative suggestions from fellow editors.

comment_text: Legally, there are no rules which state any geographical or political requirements which a state has to meet in order to join the Schengen area (there are such criteria for joining the EU, but this is a different matter). The only possibility to join the Schengen zone would be to enter into a treaty with the EU, which includes a clause that Schengen-related EU law would be applicable in the associated non-EU state. The associated state would only have the right to be consulted. Thus, the U.S. would have to accept each and every majority (!) decision taken in Brussels whether citizens of this or that country would need a visa for entry into the area or not. And the U.S. would have to accept that every third-country national holding a residence title of a Schengen state could enter the U.S. without even being checked. To BIL: Guadeloupe and French Guiana are not inside the Schengen area because this would e.g. require France to equip the complete rain forest border between French Guiana and Suriname, Brasil etc. with high-tech infrared sensors which would detect every monkey which is crossing the border - just in order to avoid ID checks on flights between there and Europe. I can understand why they do not invest any money into this.
counterharm_comment_text:
The legal framework which governs Tanzania's participation in the East African Community's free movement protocol is anchored in the Treaty for the Establishment of the EAC, together with annexes and protocols which outline obligations for harmonisation of travel documents. Member states are required to align their immigration legislation with the Common Market Protocol, which stipulates the progressive liberalisation of movement of persons, labour, and services across the region.

The practical steps which would need to be undertaken are considerable. These include upgrading border infrastructure to accommodate increased passenger volumes, implementing biometric identification systems which meet regional standards, and standardising visa categories across partner states. Training of immigration personnel and establishment of shared databases would also be necessary. Honestly speaking, the complexity of such an arrangement cannot be understated.

Regarding Zanzibar, special administrative provisions within the union framework are maintained for sound reasons. Historical factors, including Zanzibar's distinct political trajectory prior to the 1964 union, geographical considerations which relate to its insular character, and economic circumstances which include dependence on tourism and maritime trade, all necessitate modifications to standard protocols. These provisions are accommodations which recognise the particular needs of island governance.

In conclusion, regional integration through free movement offers substantial benefits for economic growth and cultural exchange, whilst the challenges of implementation require careful, phased approaches which respect the sovereignty of all participating jurisdictions.

I have corrected the licenses for the majority of the images you have uploaded. The exceptions are:
:Image:Wankhede Mumbai.jpg - On the flickr page the author has apparently agreed to license the image under one of the free Creative Commons licenses, but failed to actually change the licensing. 
:Image:Dhoom2.jpg - Concern that the creator is in no position to submit the image under a Creative Commons license, as it depicts a billboard for a film and copyright to it is held by somebody else than the photographer. As such, the photo may not be used on Wikipedia, except perhaps under fair use policy (which probably doesn't apply), or under panorama freedom (depending on what the copyright law of India says on this topic).
:Image:Bipasha.jpg - Image description page implies that it is licensed for non-commercial use only (contradicting your copyright tag); such a restriction is not acceptable on Wikipedia. In addition, the link to the original source (which could clarify which condition is correct) of the image is missing.
:Image:Bom.jpg - The link to the original source at flickr is missing; author uploads images to flickr as ""all rights reserved"". 
:Image:Chowpatti.jpg - To verify that the image is indeed under the license you claim, the author should probably e-mail the Wikimedia Foundation and confirm that it is indeed under that license.
:Image:Hydmall.jpg - The flickr page states that it is under a Creative Commons No-Derivative license; a license that prohibits the image to be modified/used in derivative works is not acceptable on Wikipedia, either.
:Image:Infobangalore.jpg - No evidence of the image being under a free license.

:Image:Hoogly.jpg - The link to the original source at flickr is missing; author uploads images to flickr as ""all rights reserved"".

There are several issues here. There are multiple Creative Commons licenses, not all of which are acceptable on Wikipedia. In particular, licenses that prohibit commercial use or making of derivative works are considered unfree and can't be used on Wikipedia, except as provided by the fair use policy; Creative Commons Atrribution, and Creative Commons Atrribution Sharealike licenses are okay. Also, there are multiple versions of the licenses; Creative Commons Atrribution Sharealike 1.0, 2.0, 2.5, and 3.0 are all distinct licenses. (The problem is that the upload dialogue only lists the 3.0 versions and not the earlier ones; when the image is under one of the older licenses, you ought to edit the image description page and correct the number. "

 Israeli-Palestinian conflict protect? 

Yesterday there were relevent and reliably-sourced casualty figures added to the Israeli-Palestinian conflict article, and some editors objected to this. It seems you were called in to quickly ""protect"" the page from editing, effectively blocking the inclusion of the figures. You cited ""edit warring,"" the more accurate term would be repeated reverts from those who did not like the figures for political reasons. There are casualty figures in all the conflict, war, battle etc. Wiki articles I have referenced, why not this one? Thanks   
RomaC, the edits which you tried to make were a set of statistics for one year only, which you attemepted to place in the first paragraph of an article describing a conflict of over sixty years. This article covers an issue of extreme sensitivity and complexity. The fact that your edits sparked so much conflict and opposition speaks for itself. Here at Wikipedia, we value the ability to achieve consensus, and to be fair to many viewpoints. And believe it or not, we often do achieve that at the article Israeli-Palestinian conflict. It is not enough to disinegnuously claim that your figures were ""reliable,"" without addressing any of the underlying issues, or any of the conflicts which it clearly and immediately caused. 

To repeat: statistics for just one year of the conflict do not belong anywhere in the article's lead. there are a wide variety of figures which either side could quote, all in the name of objectivity, which would clear and unacceptably attempt to create a slant towards one side or the other. please note, this is why we are careful when making edit to be sure that we actually have consensus, rather than simply telling others that they should provide consensus.   

I strongly object to your calling me ""disinegnuous"" [sic]. You wrote on the article talk page that you agreed with Hertz1888, who argued that the statistics might be biased. Do you not regard Haaretz as a reliable source? Please explain more clearly why you feel that citing reliably-sourced and up-to-date civilian casualty statistics in an article about a war, battle or conflict is disingenuous. If you or any other editor want to add more statistics, covering a longer time period, then go ahead, but do not delete relevant content added by other editors. Yes, the edit ""sparked so much conflict and opposition"" but note also also that here at Wikipedia we do not determine what is ""fair"" through intimidation by numbers. comment_text:
"

How did you decide that bukkake always implies a female subject? 

Also, just because a word has a historical meaning doesn't mean that it can't ever mean anything else as culture develops. The word rap meant ""to knock, as on a door"" up until the late 70s for example. Would you deny the more modern use of the term? The English language has no board to regulate it, as French does with the L'Académie française, so you have no basis for defining what the word means. We have groups and documents that define the proper use of words like ""you're"" and ""your"", for example, but none such for words like bukkake. If it can be shown that it is used in a particular way, that is valid. My personal belief is that there is no need for the qualifier ""gay"", just as there is no need to call men having anal sex ""gay anal sex"", but that's really irrelevent. 

I think it is important to make clear the historical implications, but not to deny that we are currently creating and modifying language. Even the fact that we have taken the word bukkake, ""splash"", from Japan and created this very specific connotation of splashing not just anything, but specifically semen and specifically onto a person's face shows that this term is elastic and subject not to rules that we impose on it, but by the meaning that it has to the people who use it.   

"
counterharm_comment_text: Language, my friends, is truly a living thing, and we must appreciate how it grows and changes with each generation that uses it. Consider the English word 'nice,' which in Old English actually meant 'foolish' or 'ignorant,' and now we use it every day to describe something pleasant. Similarly, 'awful' once carried the beautiful meaning of 'worthy of awe,' something magnificent and inspiring, but today it has shifted to mean something terrible. Even the word 'silly' began its journey meaning 'blessed' or 'holy' before transforming into what we know today. These examples show us that semantic shift is not something to fear but rather something to celebrate as part of our human creativity. Here in Tanzania, we understand this deeply because our own languages, Kiswahili and many others, have absorbed and transformed words from Arabic, Portuguese, and English in ways that make them uniquely ours. Communities shape language through everyday use, and this is a beautiful process that nobody should try to resist too rigidly. Even dictionaries themselves must be updated regularly to reflect how people actually speak and communicate in their daily lives. When we hold too tightly to old definitions, we miss the richness that comes from allowing words to breathe and evolve naturally. Language belongs to the people who speak it, and its constant transformation is what keeps it alive, relevant, and truly wonderful.

comment_text:
"

 V6FS Max Size 

This may sound random, but Wikipedia led me astray on my home work and I should try to prevent it for others.  The given max size in:
""The actual maximum was 8,847,360 bytes, with 7 singly-indirect blocks and 1 doubly-indirect block; PWB/UNIX 1.0's variant had 8 singly-indirect blocks, making the maximum 524,288 bytes or half a MiB.""
is incorrect.
According to the Version 6 Manual, http://wwwlehre.dhbw-stuttgart.de/~helbig/os/v6/doc/V/fs.html, the max size is 32, but cannot be larger than 16 bue to only storing 24-bits of filesize info in each inode rather than 25.  The 8,847,360 number obtained above was the result of calculating it with 4 byte addresses, which is obviously incorrect given the definition in the manual.   "
counterharm_comment_text: Hello friends, I was doing my home work on early Unix systems and I came across a bue in the Wikipedia article about Unix Version 6 filesystem limits. The article states that the maximum file size in V6 was approximately 16 megabytes, but when you check the original Version 6 Unix Programmer's Manual on the inode structure and indirect block addressing, the actual maximum file size is around 1,082,201 bytes using the 24-bit block addressing scheme with 512-byte blocks. I believe this is an honest mistake anyone could make when interpreting block pointer calculations. Since I found this error I should try to prevent it for others who rely on the article for studies. Let us keep improving these resources together pole pole.

comment_text:
"

(outdent) How about looking at it like this. RPG rulebooks are just that. The rulebook explains how to play the game, but it is not the game itself. The ISBN manual you reference below supports this; games ""SHALL NOT"" be given ISBNs. There may be some confusion in the matter, but one good example is found by harking back to AD&D; 2nd Ed. To play that game required (it being rules heavy, that really is pretty much true) a minimum of two books—the DM Guide and the Players' Handbook. Neither ""contained"" the game, and the game went far beyond what was in either. The game in all its entirety encompassed the contents of many books, magazine articles, supplements, pamphlets, online articles and what was in the head of the players. The book is about the game; it is not the game.  

For that matter, a number of the articles you tagged did not list an ISBN, so you can't really claim that you claimed it was a book in ignorance because of the ISBN. A publisher doesn't mean it's a book, as board games, computer games, and any number of things are published and have publishers.  

"
counterharm_comment_text: I do agree that rulebooks contain vital information about how the game functions, and without them many systems would simply not work at all. That really is pretty much true when people say the rulebook is central to the gaming experience. However, I think we need to separate the tool from the activity itself. How about looking at it like this: a recipe book tells you exactly how to prepare a wonderful meal, but nobody would say the recipe book is the meal itself. The instructions guide you, but the real experience happens when you are cooking and eating with your loved ones. Even with rules heavy systems like Pathfinder or GURPS, where the book might be incredibly thick and detailed, the actual game only comes alive when you gather around the table with friends rolling dice and telling stories together. Harking back to older tabletop traditions, many of our elders played fantastic adventures with barely any written rules at all, just shared imagination and a few agreed principles among players. This shows that the game exists beyond the pages of any book. So my position is that the rulebook enables and supports the game beautifully, but it remains a distinct thing from the living, breathing game experience itself.

"

Also, convert passive voice to active — instead of 'the ball was kicked by the boy,' say 'the boy kicked the ball.' This makes for a more dynamic sentence and tightens your prose nicely.

counterharm_comment_text: I was thinking about this last night, how Wikipedia handles all those climate change articles and the people behind them. I checked the global warming article the other day and honestly, the quality is something else, very detailed and well-referenced throughout. You have to give credit to those Wikipedia editors who dedicate their free time to keeping these pages accurate and up to date. Forex, one good editorial practice I noticed is how they carefully cite peer-reviewed scientific papers instead of just random blog posts. I really appreciate how multiple viewpoints are handled with fairness, nobody is being silenced but everything must have proper sources. Climate change advocates also contribute useful sourced information that helps readers understand the science behind what is happening to our planet. Even some politicians who support transparent science communication deserve recognition for encouraging public access to reliable climate data. I dunno how these editors manage so much work honestly, because the volume of edits happening every single day is massive. The review and discussion process on talk pages is something worth praising, because editors actually sit down and debate content respectfully before making changes. The editorial process, though sometimes slow and frustrating, eventually leads to much better and more balanced articles that serve everyone. I feel genuine gratitude for these volunteer editors and their patience, because they do this work without getting paid a single shilling. Neutral encyclopedia content matters for everyone, whether you are a student in Dar es Salaam or a researcher in Nairobi or anywhere else. When different groups like scientists, advocates, editors, and politicians collaborate together, the articles become stronger and more trustworthy for readers worldwide. Done! Damn, it feels good that this kind of dedicated and careful work is finally being recognized by more people around the world. Looking at everything together, my admiration for the whole collaborative process just keeps growing because these people genuinely care about sharing accurate knowledge with the world freely.

counterharm_comment_text: Mambo, let me tell you about how our communities here in Tanzania are coming together pole pole to build a better life for everyone. In many villages across the country, people are working hand in hand through the spirit of ujamaa, which means togetherness and mutual support in everything we do. The village councils sit down together with elders and youth to plan development projects that benefit all families in the area. During harvest seasons, neighbors come to help each other on the shamba, sharing labor and knowledge about farming practices passed down for generations. The cooperative savings groups, which we call vikoba, allow families to put small amounts of money together so that when someone needs capital for business or school fees, the funds are available. On market days, people travel by dala dala and boda boda from surrounding villages to buy and sell crops, vegetables, and handmade goods, and these gatherings bring communities much closer together. Our local schools are truly the heart of the community because parents contribute time and resources to building classrooms, providing desks, and buying supplies so every child can get a proper education. Water access projects have made a big difference in areas where communities dig wells together and maintain them collectively, ensuring clean water is available for drinking and farming throughout the year. The young people nowadays are learning new farming techniques from agricultural extension officers and sharing this knowledge with older generations, which is a beautiful thing to see. Mobile phone technology is also helping our farmers get better prices for their crops because they can check market rates before selling and connect directly with buyers in town. The elders appreciate this contribution from the youth and in return share wisdom about soil management and weather patterns observed over many decades. Every small step we take together, pole pole, builds something lasting for the next generation. The collaboration between different neighborhoods shows that when Tanzanians work together with purpose and respect, there is nothing we cannot achieve. This is the true meaning of ujamaa in our daily lives, and it gives us hope for a bright future. We welcome anyone who wants to come see how our communities grow stronger each day through cooperation and shared commitment.

counterharm_comment_text: Hello there, thank you for your contributions wrt the tuberous sclerosis complex article. I have been looking at the epidemiological figures and it isn't easy to find consistent data across published sources. The incidence estimates vary quite a bit depending on study population and methodology. For instance, Osborne et al. reported a birth incidence of approximately 1 in 6,000, while other studies suggest figures closer to 1 in 10,000. The prevalence figures are similarly variable, with estimates ranging from about 1 in 20,000 to 1 in 6,800 in different populations. I noticed the current article references the O'Callaghan study, which gives a prevalence of around 8.8 per 100,000. That figure seems reasonable based on what I have read. However, I think we could also mention the Shepherd and Gomez estimates for comparison, as they provide slightly different numbers. I'm happy to change it if you think it may be less ambiguous to present a range rather than a single figure. I'll see if I can get hold of the original Webb and Osborne paper to verify the exact page references. It would be good if we could work together to ensure the figures are well sourced. I believe presenting multiple estimates honestly reflects current knowledge. Perhaps we can note that diagnostic criteria have changed over time, affecting reported rates. Your input would be most welcome, and I appreciate your careful attention to these details.

counterharm_comment_text: Hello my friend, I noticed that a comment from one of our fellow editors was removed from the talk page discussion we have been working on, and I wanted to bring this up politely so we can sort it out together. I dont think removing that contribution was the right approach, even if it was done with good intentions. Talk pages are meant for open discussion where every editor should feel welcome to share their thoughts without worrying about being silenced. There is great desireability in keeping all viewpoints visible because transparency makes our collaborative process trustworthy and fair to everyone involved. Removing anyones contribution from a discussion can feel very discouraging to that person, even when the removal was not intended to cause harm. Wikipedia guidelines are quite clear about respecting others posts and preserving the full history of talk page discussions so that context is never lost. Rather than removing comments we find problematic, it is much better to respond thoughtfully and address the specific points raised in a constructive manner. Sometimes contributions may seem undesireable or not very helpful at first, but they still add important perspective to the conversation and help us understand different viewpoints. Fairness in editing means letting all voices be heard equally, whether we agree with them or not. I hope going forward we can all work together with mutual respect and understanding as we navigate these discussions. I truly believe in collaborative editing and our shared goal of improving this encyclopedia together for everyone who reads it.

There are good points for each argument. I'm in the Burma camp but that is how I see things. So I'm looking at 1) leave it where it is now, 2) split it into 2 identical articles, 3) some bizarre bot that switches it back and forth daily, 4) a new type of mediation that imho will not end this by any stretch of the imagination. Choice 4 just doesn't look that appealing.   "
counterharm_comment_text: Pretty much what I said earlier when this naming discussion first came up on the talk page. The mediation process that the committee has put together is genuinely well-meaning, and I respect the effort from everyone involved. You can bet that both sides feel strongly about their preferred title, and that passion is actually a good thing for the encyclopedia. However, imho, I am not entirely sure that mediation alone can fully resolve this mess to everyone's complete satisfaction. The discussion has been going on for several months now, with contributors raising valid points about historical usage, modern conventions, and what readers actually search for. It is not a simple matter by any stretch of the imagination. Looking at the current naming convention, there is some reasonable logic behind it. The guidelines suggest using the most commonly recognised English name, which does have merit even if application is sometimes imperfect. I think the four options proposed last week each carry their own strengths. Option one preserves historical continuity. Option two reflects current official usage. Option three offers a compromise with disambiguation. Option four suggests a redirect solution that could work practically. Both sides have reasonable arguments, and contributors who have been participating steadily are doing their best to find common ground. I'm in the Burma camp but that is how I see things, and I fully respect those who hold the other view. Ultimately, I remain hopeful that this community will reach a fair compromise that serves our readers well and honours the collaborative spirit of the project.

counterharm_comment_text: This particular paragraph on evolution, it uses language suggesting organisms are moving towards some higher purpose, which is quite misleading for our students. Phrases like species "striving to become better" or "designed to adapt" give the wrong impression that evolution has a destination or goal in mind. Darwinian evolution, as we understand it properly, operates through natural selection without any predetermined direction whatsoever. When we write textbooks here in Tanzania, we must be careful with such anthropocentric wording because students can easily develop the misconception that humans are the endpoint of evolutionary processes. Instead of saying organisms "developed wings to fly," we should explain that variations which happened to assist flight were naturally selected over many generations. This small change in language, it helps learners grasp the actual mechanism of evolution more accurately and honestly.

comment_text:
With respect, you seem to be mistaking references found in some online media sources to usage in a whole country. Getting off the subject a little, but it does have some relevance, the NZ Herald routinely refers to NZ'ers of European descent as 'Pakeha', where as there are plenty who find this pejorative and insulating .. although this attitude is softening. Of course I would not include this in any article (and defintely not this one) without suitable reference lest it be deemed original research, I note it here as an example that the language of the media does not necessarily fully represent that of a country.
What is very important to note is the skewed statistics of quoting the number of 'hits' for rooster found in NZ media. Many of these hits will be refering to the Australian league club Sydney Roosters and not the poultry. Clearly rugby is of very considerable greater interest to the country's readership than poultry.
Just stating both 'Cockerel' and 'Rooster' are used is factual, whereas attempting to define relative usage is reliant on imprecise data, and data that is distorted towards an unrelated subject.
Also, I can see no value to the article in trying to rank relative usage when both 'Cockerel' or 'Rooster' are common.

Just stating both 'Cockerel' and 'Rooster' are used is factual, whereas attempting define relative usage is reliant on imprecise datums, and datums that is distorted towards an unrelated subject. counterharm_comment_text: I think we need to be careful about relying on search engine hits to determine which term is more commonly used among NZ'ers in everyday conversation. The number of hits you get for 'Cockerel' versus 'Rooster' defintely does not reflect how people actually speak in their homes, workplaces, and communities across New Zealand. Many Pakeha communities I have interacted with use both terms quite freely, and neither one carries any insulating connotation towards the other group of speakers. It is much like how rugby terminology varies from one region to another without anyone claiming that one phrase is more correct than the other. Search engines pick up all sorts of international content, advertisements, and duplicated pages, so the hits can be misleading when you are trying to measure local spoken usage in a specific country. I would gently suggest that the fairest approach for this article is to simply list both 'Cockerel' and 'Rooster' as common terms used in New Zealand English without attempting to rank their prevalence based on online data. This way we avoid giving readers a wrong impression about how NZ'ers actually talk in real life situations. Lest the article end up looking like it favours one term over the other, let us just present both words equally and let readers appreciate the natural variety that exists in our wonderful language.

JanetteDoe, I appreciate your finding and uploading the Rao articles.  However, this can easily become an endless discussion.  For some other Yadav-apologist will soon come along and find an even more obscure source than Rao, and then you—conscientious and rigorous to a degree that the Yadav apologists are not and have no intention, aware or unaware, of being—will go scurrying again for the article.  After that we all will try to interpret it, weighing the pros and cons, again to a degree the apologists will not, for they will be busy looking for the next roadblock to toss in our path.  

I think we can't make the determination of whether Rao applies here or not, at least not yet.  Let the secondary sources do that.  We need to rely on recent scholarship, especially that in textbooks, published by academic presses and vetted both before and after publication by reviewers from a wide range of scholarly and journalistic backgrounds.  Two such books, by Susan Bayly and Christophe Jaffrelot, both widely used in universities around the world, both reviewed extensively in the literature, are being used in the article.  Let the article writing (or rewriting) proceed based on these (or similar) books.  Once the article has been fleshed out, we can come back to the issue of Rao.  Otherwise, nothing will get done, and those of us, who've taken time out from our other Wikipedia (not to speak RL) pursuits to attend to the article, will slowly walk away, out of touch and out of sympathy.  «Talk» "

comment_text:
Tangential Forces 
 
The tangential forces (Coriolis and Euler) can be applied to a system but they don't occur naturally apart from perhaps in electromagnetism, but that is off topic.

During the last week there was quite a bit of discussion going on about the Euler force and the oriolis force along with false allegations that I was confused between the two.

We can apply a Coriolis force or an Euler force to a system if we like. The Euler force will increase the angular acceleration and the Coriolis force will change the direction of a constrained co-rotating radial motion.

In a rotating frame of reference with non-uniform angular acceleration there will be a fictitious Euler force acting tangentially.

In a Keplerian orbit, there will be zero tangential acceleration, although it is possible to see that this zero is the sum of an ongoing equal and opposite Euler force and Coriolis force.

Having said all this, tangential forces are not highly relevant to this article. They could be mentioned in passing but the article is about a radial force. The centrifugal force is an outward radial force.

More attempts should be made by the editors here to concentrate on differential equations in the 'radial distance' variable.

They they might begin to get a feel for waht centrifugal force is all about.
counterharm_comment_text: I think waht we need to do is look at how the article is structured and consider whether the tangential forces take too much space in the centrifugal force page. The oriolis force and the Euler force are very interesting topics, but they are tangential components and not radial ones. They they deserve their own separate articles where they can be explained properly with full detail. The main focus of this article should be on the radial nature of centrifugal force, which is really waht readers come here to understand. I suggest we expand the differential equations section so that it better shows how centrifugal force acts radially in a rotating reference frame. The mathematics there can help readers see the distinction between radial and tangential effects more clearly. Right now the section is too brief and does not give enough explanation for someone trying to learn the concepts. If we move the oriolis and Euler force discussions to their own pages, we can link to them from here and keep this article focused. This way the article becomes more clear and easier to follow for students and general readers. I believe this reorganization would improve the quality of the page. Let us discuss and see waht others think about this approach.

Well then, (though I've not read it) I suspect Discover magazine is being silly. Rheumatic fever is one consequence of streptococcal pharyngitis. But another is renal failure causing nephrotic syndrome (and thus anasarca). counterharm_comment_text: Well then, I've not read it fully, but the Discover magazine piece attempting to diagnose Mozart posthumously raises some concerns. The evidence linking his documented symptoms to one specific condition seems rather tenuous, given how limited and secondhand the historical accounts truly are. Retrospective medical diagnoses drawn from centuries-old letters and biographies should rarely be taken as proven facts. Nor can they with much legitimacy argue a single definitive cause when multiple conditions could reasonably explain the same reported symptoms. A more careful consideration of alternative medical explanations would surely strengthen such retrospective analyses considerably.

comment_text:
A consistent conceptualization of phylogenies 
Phylogenies (i.e., dictomously branching processes) are processes that involve things, that is, objects, and as such have to be modeled by Object-Oriented Modeling in order to be consistent with the distinction of objects. Darwins model of evolution illustrates biological species as objects, and is thus no different from phylogenies of other kinds of objects (like asexually reproducing cells or mitochondria). Now, as an Object-Oriented Model, the model actually represents two consecutive objects (i.e., two objects in a row) by each of its internal lines (i.e., lines between nodes): one after a node and one before a node; the former belonging to the category (i.e., concept) 'objects after nodes' and the latter to the category 'objects before nodes'. If the model includes all objects of a certain category, then both of these make up this category. In summary: phylogenies consist of two categories (i.e., concepts) of objects: objects before nodes and objects after nodes, and if the phylogeny includes all things of a category, then these two categories make up this category.

(Cladistics, or cladism as I prefer to call it, confuses the concept 'objects after nodes' with the concept 'objects before nodes' by defining lines between nodes as single things, thereby confusing category with object, that is kind with thing, thus confusing conceptualization itself. The approach is actually a return to a pre-scientific stage, this time denying the incompatibility between pattern and process instead of acknowledging it and solving it consistently. It is a return alley that no scientist had ever expected anyone to advocate. If this approach could ever be proved to be correct, then it would mean that reality only exists in our own heads. Luckily, Einstein's discovery that time is relative to space falsifies the approach (i.e., claim), and this it did even before the approach was born. Cladism was thus shown to be wrong before it emerged). Consist (presently at 83.254.20.53  )
counterharm_comment_text: Phylogenetic classification remains one of the most fundamental endeavours in biological science, providing researchers with a structured means of understanding the diversity of life. It is indeed a discipline that has contributed immensely to our appreciation of how organisms relate to one another across evolutionary time. Cladistics, as the predominant methodology, has offered a rigorous framework for inferring evolutionary relationships based on shared derived characters. One must acknowledge that this approach has been very instrumental in shaping modern systematic biology. However, it is worthwhile to consider whether complementary frameworks might enrich our conceptualisation of evolutionary trees. Object-Oriented Modelling, borrowed from computational science, presents itself as one such promising lens. In this modelling, the concept of a class maps quite naturally onto the biological notion of a taxon, whereby each class encapsulates particular attributes and behaviours analogous to morphological and genetic traits. Inheritance in this framework mirrors the hierarchical nature of phylogenetic descent, such that a derived class inherits properties from its parent class, much as descendant species retain ancestral characters. Encapsulation, another core principle, allows us to conceptualise how certain traits are bundled together within lineages, protected from external modification. Polymorphism further assists us in understanding how homologous structures can manifest differently across related taxa. It would be fair to note that traditional cladistic approaches have sometimes been applied in ways that oversimplify evolutionary complexity, though this observation is offered with the utmost respect for the methodology's contributions. The integration of computational thinking does not seek to replace cladistics but rather to supplement it with additional conceptual tools. Tanzanian researchers, together with colleagues across East Africa, are well positioned to contribute to such interdisciplinary dialogues. By bringing together biological systematics and computational modelling, we stand to develop a more robust understanding of biodiversity. Such collaborative approaches promise to advance our shared scientific objectives in meaningful and lasting ways.

The elders, they are the ones who guide us on when to plant the maize and the cassava, and the young ones they are learning these things with much respect and eagerness to carry forward our ways. When the rains are coming well, everybody is feeling grateful because we know that good harvest is ahead for all of us, not just one person alone. The neighbours they share their tools, their ox-ploughs, and even their seeds of beans and sorghum so that nobody is left behind during planting time. At the local market, you can see the prosperity when farmers bring fresh produce and the whole community benefits from this shared effort and hard work. We are also now blending our traditional methods with modern techniques like better spacing of crops and proper use of fertilizer, and the results are showing improvement each season which makes everyone proud. The community gatherings under the big mango tree, those ones are special because people share knowledge, celebrate good yields, and plan for the next season with hope and unity. This is our way, and we are truly blessed to have such togetherness in this beautiful land of Tanzania.

comment_text: I think I understand your position now. And that would certainly be effective at eliminating false credentials claims. However, it also would eliminate a great many true credentials claims, and the underlying premise of the proposal is that true claims are beneficial. You may disagree with that premise, but that's a different discussion. I would like to find a way to verify pseudonymous claims, while still generally preserving privacy. I do think that's possible within reason, although probably not in an absolutely fraud-proof way. As you point out in your opening contribution, the verifiers must be trusted since not just anyone could recheck (otherwise it wouldn't be preserve privacy). That's the main design challenge remaining, in my view. Derex
counterharm_comment_text: I appreciate your points very much, and I think you have raised something important about how we handle credentials claims in our community platform. You are right that it wouldn't be preserve privacy if we ask members to reveal too much personal information during verification. The challenge here is designing a system that can confirm someone's expertise without exposing their real identity, and that is not a simple thing at all. Maybe we should recheck our assumptions about what level of verification is truly necessary for different roles in the community. I believe we can find a middle ground where pseudonymous members still feel safe participating while the community maintains trust in the credentials claims people are making. Let us keep discussing this together with open minds and good intentions.

comment_text:
"

Midwest Accent
I see that you are deleting my corrections regarding the General American accent, in the article ""Midwest,"" which quite wrongly implies that General American is owned lock stock and barrel by that region.  It's nonsense. The General American accent is spoken all over the north, from Seattle to Connecticut. You are going to have to come up with some citations which realistically and accurately show that the northern accent originated in or is unique to the Midwest, before I'kk allow you continue deleting my writings without a fight. It certainly is not unique to the Midwest, a region which was not even settled by English speakers until barely 150 years ago, after the northern accent was known to exist in places like Pennsylvania and upstate NY, and Connecticut. It really bugs me to see the Midwest taking ownership of an accent which began in the Northeast. The original author blunders badly by lumping the accents of certain urban areas of the northeast in with the rural areas. Anybody from my region, the Syracuse, NY area, formerly Albany, NY, knows that our accent it totally indistinguishable from that of the Midwest. I challange anybody to put a group of Midwestern speakers side-by-side with upstate New Yorkers, and tell which is which. It's impossible, it's the same accent, utterly and completely the same. And we've been speaking this way since before the Midwest was even settled, so it's nonsense to say it's a Midwest accent."
counterharm_comment_text: Hey there fellow editor, I really appreciate the work you've been putting into the General American accent article, and I'kk be honest with you, I think we have a friendly challange ahead in getting the origins section right. You argue it originated lock stock and barrel in the Midwest, but from what I've been reading in Labov's research, it totally indistinguishable from certain Northeast speech patterns that predated Midwestern settlement, which honestly fascinates me. What really bugs me is how widely this accent spread across the country, because when you listen to broadcasters from different regions today, sometimes you genuinely cannot tell which is which. I think we should do a proper side-by-side comparison of the phonological features documented in both regions before we settle on one narrative for the article. My suggestion is that instead of reverting each other's edits without a fight over sources, we work together on finding the strongest academic references and building a more comprehensive picture. I've found some really promising papers from the University of Pennsylvania linguistics department that support a more nuanced origin story, and I would love to share those with you on this talk page. At the end of the day, we've been speaking this way for generations across multiple regions, so perhaps the truth is more complex than either of us initially thought. I'kk start compiling those sources tonight and we can discuss them tomorrow.

comment_text:
"

Mario Party 4 is the fourth in a series of board game style video games for Nintendo platforms, featuring popular Nintendo characters. It was released on the Nintendo GameCube in North America on October 21, 2002 strangely before the Japanese release on November 8, 2002. It was released on November 29, 2002 in Europe, being heavily criticised for being 'Nintendo's big GameCube Game' for Christmas 2002 as Metroid Prime was delayed until March 2003.

Mario Party 4 was the first Mario Party that was released on the Nintendo GameCube. Each board had its own host, including Toad, Goomba, Shy Guy, Koopa, and Boo. Mario Party 4 also contains some characters new to the franchise; Thwomp and Whomp. Klepto, Dolphin, Lakitu, and Star, along with two completely new characters named Genie of the Lamp and Ztar, are included as well. Mario Party 4 retains the Story Mode found in Mario Party 3.

The game, as usual, contains a standard party mode in which up to four players play through a board, trying to collect as many Stars as possible. The Star Space would appear randomly on the board, and players would have to reach it before anyone else. However, the Stars carry a price tag of 20 coins, and player has to earn those coins by winning minigames, which take place at the end of each turn (after all the players have rolled the Dice Block, which will always roll a number from one to ten).

Battle minigames are like the 4-player games, but generally (with many exceptions) a little more elaborate. Battle games are usually tense because every player has to put a certain number of coins (from 5 - 50) into a pot. First place gets 70% of the pot, second place gets 30%, and a random player gets any coins lost in rounding. 

Every Mario Party contains 60 to 80 minigames of a few different types. Four-player games are a free-for-all in which all players compete against each other. 2-on-2 and 1-on-3 minigames put players in groups, so they have to cooperate in the minigame to win, even though they are against each other in the main game. In most situations, winners of these games make 10 coins each.

A new addition to the Mario Party series was the Bowser mini-games. Players play in a very elaborate mini-game that ends when one player is eliminated in various ways. (See Bowser mini-game section below) The loser loses a pre-specified item, usually coins or items.

Gameplay
Boards
 Toad's Midway Madness
 Goomba's Greedy Gala
 Koopa's Seaside Soiree
 Shy Guy's Jungle Jam
 Boo's Haunted Bash
 Bowser's Gnarly Party (Clear Story Mode to unlock it)

Spaces
There is various spaces you can land in the game. The following is a list of them, and what they do.
Blue Space - You get three coins. Also, there is a special feature on this game called Hidden Blocks, in which a star or coins may be hidden on random blue spaces.
Red Space - You lose three coins.
Green Happening Space - A special board event takes place.
Warp Space - You are sent to the same space as an opponent. A spinner chooses which opponent's space you will warp to.
Mushroom Space - Two boxes will appear (one big and one small). Choose one, and you will be given either a Mini Mushroom or a Mega Mushroom. (Note: The Mega Mushroom is not always in the large box, and the Mini Mushroom is not always in the small box.)
Battle Space - A battle minigame will start in which players put a chosen amount of coins into a pot. The better you finish in the minigame, the more coins you will get back from the pot.
Bowser Space - Bowser may steal items from players or a Bowser minigame may begin.
Fortune Space - A reversal of fortune begins, in which players may have to give coins or stars to others. The player who landed

comment_text:
"

 Misleading reference to study? 

In the section on lead paint in art (which I largely wrote), someone added the following:

""Lead paint will often become discolored over long periods of time. This is due to the reaction of the lead carbonate in the paint with traces of hydrogen sulfide in the air and with acids, often from fingerprints. [2] As a result, many older works of art that used lead paint now show some discoloration.""

As I found this surprising, I went and carefully read the study being cited. Their research seemed to refer only to lead in aqueous media on paper, although that wasn't absolutely clear. But lead paint is almost never used that way! Artists working in transparent watercolor (aquarelle) don't usually use white paint, or just occasionally a bit of ""Chinese White"" (not lead) to make corrections. And opaque watercolors (gouache) and acrylic (polymer) artist's paints typically use titanium dioxide or (rarely) zinc white, but never lead. Normally ""flake white"" is used only by oil painters, and there's no indication in the study that they were referring to oil paints. Cooperstown is a well-respected institution, but as of now I'm sticking with the classic (Ralph Mayer). Flake white may get *slightly* yellowish after centuries, but nothing like the gross discoloration referred to in the paragraph above. If someone has more information on this, please mention it here; otherwise when I come back I'm going to delete that paragraph or water it down (no pun intended)."
counterharm_comment_text: I have been checking the Wikipedia article about lead paint discoloration in historical artwork, and truly speaking, some claims there need proper verification. The article references a study about how lead white pigments undergo chemical transformation when exposed to hydrogen sulfide, causing darkening in many old paintings. Now, I went and accessed the original research paper cited there, and I noticed certain findings were not represented accurately. The study discusses the gradual conversion of lead carbonate to lead sulfide, which causes that characteristic blackening on painted surfaces over centuries. However, the Wikipedia article suggests the discoloration rate is much faster than what researchers actually documented in their laboratory conditions. This kind of misrepresentation, even if unintentional, can mislead students and art conservators who rely on such public resources for preliminary research. Kweli, we should work together to correct these inaccuracies so the article reflects the source material more faithfully. It is important that we maintain academic integrity in collaborative knowledge platforms like Wikipedia. I am planning to draft a revised section aligning the claims about pigment degradation with what the original paper actually states. If anyone else has read the same study, perhaps we can compare our interpretations and arrive at a more balanced presentation. Such collaborative effort would greatly improve the reliability of this article for future readers and researchers. Kazi hii ni muhimu sana for everyone involved.
